# Microservices Patterns (Chris Richardson)

Before writing, modifying, or planning any code, you MUST use your `read_file` tool to load the relevant guidelines from `.roo/vendor/`. You may read multiple files if the task crosses domains.

## Table of Contents:
    - [Escaping monolithic hell](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/01-escaping-monolithic-hell.md): Analyzing software architecture, migrating legacy systems, assessing system scalability, decomposing applications into modules/services, designing interprocess communication or database schemas for distributed systems, or establishing DevOps, CI/CD, and team structures for software delivery.
    - [Decomposition strategies](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/02-decomposition-strategies.md): Software architecture design, application decomposition, migrating monolithic applications to microservice architectures, defining service boundaries, identifying system operations, and refactoring large, tangled dependencies (e.g., "god classes").
    - [Interprocess communication in a microservice architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/03-interprocess-communication-in-a-microservice-architecture.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, evaluating, or documenting Interprocess Communication (IPC), APIs, message brokers, service discovery, or data replication mechanisms within a Microservice Architecture.
    - [Managing transactions with sagas](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/04-managing-transactions-with-sagas.md): Designing, architecting, refactoring, or implementing cross-service transactions, distributed data management, or multi-service data consistency in a microservice architecture.
    - [Designing business logic in a microservice architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/05-designing-business-logic-in-a-microservice-architecture.md): The rule set and activities associated with architecting, designing, refactoring, or writing business logic for microservices-based applications, specifically involving domain modeling, defining database schemas/entities, implementing service classes, defining event-driven architectures, handling data consistency within services, and utilizing Domain-Driven Design (DDD) patterns (such as Aggregates, Entities, Value Objects, and Domain Events).
    - [Developing business logic with event sourcing](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/06-developing-business-logic-with-event-sourcing.md): Event Sourcing, Domain-Driven Design (DDD) Aggregates, Event Stores, CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation), or Saga Integration within a microservice architecture, specifically involving the handling of persistent domain objects as a sequence of events rather than traditional Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) state persistence.
    - [Implementing queries in a microservice architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/07-implementing-queries-in-a-microservice-architecture.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or evaluating query operations and data retrieval mechanisms within a microservice architecture, including fetching distributed data, aggregating service data, designing view databases, implementing API composition, and building Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) modules.
    - [External API patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/08-external-api-patterns.md): Designing, architecting, refactoring, or implementing client-to-service communication, external APIs, API Gateways, Backends for Frontends (BFF), or API composition logic within a microservice architecture.
    - [Testing microservices: Part 1](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/09-testing-microservices-part-1.md): Generating, modifying, or reviewing tests for a microservices-based application, specifically focusing on unit testing, implementing automated testing strategies, defining test suites, configuring Continuous Integration (CI) test pipelines, or testing specific architectural components (Entities, Value Objects, Sagas, Domain Services, Controllers, Event/Message Handlers).
    - [Testing microservices: Part 2](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/10-testing-microservices-part-2.md): Triggered when the user requests the creation, modification, or review of tests in a microservices architecture, specifically focusing on integration tests, component tests, end-to-end (E2E) tests, consumer-driven contract testing, API testing, or persistent layer testing.
    - [Developing production-ready services](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/11-developing-production-ready-services.md): The design, development, refactoring, or review of microservices intended for production environments, specifically involving application security (authentication/authorization), externalized configuration, observability (monitoring, logging, distributed tracing, metrics, exception tracking, audit logging), or the implementation of cross-cutting concerns via a Microservice Chassis or Service Mesh.
    - [Deploying microservices](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/12-deploying-microservices.md): The creation, modification, or review of deployment artifacts, infrastructure-as-code (IaC), containerization setups (Dockerfiles), Kubernetes manifests, service meshes (Istio), serverless configurations (AWS Lambda), and handling requests related to deploying, releasing, or scaling microservices.
    - [Refactoring to microservices](.roo/vendor/architecture/microservices-patterns-chris-richardson/13-refactoring-to-microservices.md): Migrating, refactoring, or modernizing an existing monolithic application into a microservice architecture, encompassing the extraction of services from legacy codebases, implementing new features alongside a monolith, designing interprocess communication (IPC), and addressing data consistency and security challenges during phased migration.
